mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows teams to manage security and compliance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for automating governance and ensuring that Kubernetes resources adhere to organizational policies. Key trade-offs include the need for proper policy definitions and potential performance impacts if overly complex policies are applied.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository for Helm.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Create a namespace for Kyverno.
> Install Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This command checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Ensure all Kyverno pods are in the 'Running' state.

steps.title: Create a sample policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/main/docs/examples/policies/policy.yaml

```

> Apply a sample policy from the Kyverno GitHub repository.

steps.title: Verify the policy
steps.description:
This command checks if the policy is applied and functioning as expected.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno

```

> List the cluster policies to verify the sample policy is present.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno and its associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstall the Kyverno release.
> Delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up existing policies. This step performs the upgrade and verifies the installation.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies to a file.
> Upgrade Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Kyverno is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kyverno pods may not have started due to insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the resource allocation and ensure the nodes have enough CPU and memory.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Policy not applied'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may have syntax errors or is not properly defined.

**Fix:**
Review the policy YAML for errors and validate it using 'kubectl apply --dry-run'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may be blocking the creation of certain resources based on its rules.

**Fix:**
Check the policy rules and adjust them if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod crashes with 'OOMKilled'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The pod is exceeding its memory limits.

**Fix:**
Increase the memory limits in the Kyverno deployment configuration.

versionNotes.changes: Improved policy validation performance and added support for new policy types.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy policy types that are no longer recommended.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update any deprecated policies to align with the new policy types.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with the output similar to 'NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE' followed by the pod names.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.
